answer: Chapter 4 of Comte's Traité de la propriété Vol I (slug: charles-comte-traite-de-la-propriete-vol-i-2c7a99, chapter 4, titled 'De l'occupation des choses') argues that occupation — the act of taking possession of a thing that has no master, with the intention of appropriating it — is a law of nature, not a product of social convention or government decree. Comte's central arguments are: (1) Occupation is the foundational means by which nations and individuals acquire the raw elements from which all property is formed; human industry transforms these elements but does not create them — it first must occupy them. (2) The conventional juristic theory that all men originally had equal rights over everything, and that a universal convention produced exclusive property rights, is a 'chimera' — such a universal convention is impossible and unintelligible. (3) Occupation is a necessity of human nature: progress is impossible while lands remain uncultivated and things unappropriated; community of goods is equally unworkable; therefore appropriation by nations, families, and individuals is unavoidable. (4) The first-occupant rule is justified because the first seizer demonstrates the thing suits him better than others, despoils no one of their means of existence, and deceiving his expectation would diminish the utility of the thing. (5) Any thing that can satisfy a need and be exclusively possessed is susceptible to acquisition by occupation, whether animate or inanimate, movable or immovable. (6) The seas (as means of navigation and communication) are not susceptible to occupation because applying the principle there would restrict rather than maximise utility — though maritime nations may appropriate adjacent coastal waters for security and fishing. (7) Comte distinguishes occupation (which applies only to ownerless things and is complete by the mere act) from possession (which can apply to already-appropriated things but requires duration and certain circumstances).
